elements.sent-2411-001: Real-world image super-resolution (Real-ISR) aims to reconstruct high-resolution images from low-resolution inputs degraded by complex, unknown processes.
elements.sent-2411-002: While many Stable Diffusion (SD)-based Real-ISR methods have achieved remarkable success, their slow, multi-step inference hinders practical deployment.
elements.sent-2411-003: Recent SD-based one-step networks like OSEDiff and S3Diff alleviate this issue but still incur high computational costs due to their reliance on large pretrained SD models.
elements.sent-2411-004: This paper proposes a novel Real-ISR method, AdcSR, by distilling the one-step diffusion network OSEDiff into a streamlined diffusion-GAN model under our Adversarial Diffusion Compression (ADC) framework.
elements.sent-2411-005: We meticulously examine the modules of OSEDiff, categorizing them into two types: (1) Removable (VAE encoder, prompt extractor, text encoder, \etc) and (2) Prunable (denoising UNet and VAE decoder).
elements.sent-2411-006: Since direct removal and pruning can degrade the model's generation capability, we pretrain our pruned VAE decoder to restore its ability to decode images and employ adversarial distillation to compensate for performance loss.
elements.sent-2411-007: This ADC-based diffusion-GAN hybrid design effectively reduces complexity by 73\% in inference time, 78\% in computation, and 74\% in parameters, while preserving the modelâ€™s generation capability.
elements.sent-2411-008: Experiments manifest that our proposed AdcSR achieves competitive recovery quality on both synthetic and real-world datasets, offering up to 9.3$\times$ speedup over previous one-step diffusion-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-009: Code and models will be made available.
elements.sent-2411-010: Image super-resolution (ISR) \cite{dong2014learning,timofte2015a+,zhang2018image,liang2021swinir,liu20242dquant is a fundamental and long-standing problem in computer vision.}
elements.sent-2411-011: It aims to reconstruct the high-resolution (HR) image from a low-resolution (LR) counterpart.
elements.sent-2411-012: One line of ISR research assumes that the LR image $\x_\text{LR}$ is a bicubic-downsampled version of the HR image $\x_\text{HR}$.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, deep ISR networks trained using this assumption often struggle to generalize to real-world scenarios, where degradations are more complex and typically unknown.
elements.sent-2411-014: Another increasingly popular line of ISR research, known as real-world ISR (Real-ISR) \cite{zhang2021designing,wang2021real , employs random shufflings of degradation operations and high-order degradation processes to synthesize LR-HR training pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-015: These approaches have improved the performance of deep ISR networks in real-world scenarios.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/teaser.pdf} \caption{Comparison between our proposed AdcSR and typical one-step diffusion-based Real-ISR methods. (a) The state-of-the-art one-step diffusion network OSEDiff \cite{wu2024one} employs complete SD \cite{stabilityai} models for Real-ISR, suffering from high computational costs. (b) We distill OSEDiff (ADC-teacher) into a smaller diffusion-GAN hybrid model, AdcSR (ADC-student), under the proposed ADC framework, achieving significantly improved efficiency while maintaining competitive recovery performance.} \label{fig:teaser}

elements.sent-2411-025: To reduce complexity while maintaining recovery quality, in this paper, we propose a novel diffusion-based Real-ISR model AdcSR, which is obtained by applying our proposed adversarial diffusion compression (ADC) framework to OSEDiff.
elements.sent-2411-026: Our main idea is based on the hypothesis that, given LR input $\x_\text{LR}$ containing abundant information about the target HR image $\x_\text{HR}$, a structurally compressed version of SD-based one-step diffusion networks like OSEDiff \cite{wu2024one has a sufficient capacity to learn an effective Real-ISR mapping.}
elements.sent-2411-027: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:teaser , we remove the variational autoencoder (VAE) encoder, prompt extractor, text encoder, cross-attention (CA), and time embedding layers in the SD UNet which we find less important than other modules like self-attention (SA) layers to develop the architecture of AdcSR.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Then, we compress the remaining denoising UNet and VAE decoder using channel pruning for improved efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-029: To preserve the model's generative recovery ability while ensuring training efficiency, inspired by the success of diffusion GANs \cite{xiao2021tackling,wang2022diffusion,sauer2023adversarial,kim2023consistency,xu2024ufogen,lin2024sdxl,kang2024distilling,sauer2024fast,yin2024improved,luo2024you , we pretrain our pruned VAE decoder and introduce adversarial distillation in the feature space of VAE decoder.}

elements.sent-2411-055: Structural Compression for Latent Diffusion Models.
elements.sent-2411-056: To achieve photo-realistic image generation, large-scale latent diffusion models \cite{rombach2022high,stabilityai,podell2023sdxl are widely employed due to their powerful generative priors.}
elements.sent-2411-057: However, the deployment of these models is hindered by their high computation costs.
elements.sent-2411-058: To address this issue, a lot of works \cite{fang2023structural,castells2024edgefusion,zhu2024slimflow,zhang2024laptop,zhao2023mobilediffusion have explored compression techniques for efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-059: For example, BK-SDM \cite{kim2023bk applies block removal for SD models.}
elements.sent-2411-060: SnapFusion \cite{li2024snapfusion designs block-removed UNet and efficient VAE decoder with an improved distillation approach, achieving 8-step T2I inferences.}
elements.sent-2411-061: To our knowledge, no existing compression techniques are specifically designed for diffusion-based Real-ISR.
elements.sent-2411-062: In this work, we propose a novel method based on introduced adversarial diffusion compression (ADC).
elements.sent-2411-063: Moving beyond previous one-step approaches \cite{wang2024sinsr,wu2024one,cui2024taming,zhang2024degradation,li2024distillation , we demonstrate that, given LR image as a starting point of super-resolution, the latent encoding, prompt extraction, text-conditioned denoising, and decoding can be compressed into an optimized diffusion GAN.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1.02\textwidth]{figs/method.pdf} \caption{Illustration of the training and inference processes of AdcSR, an instantiation of our ADC framework applied to OSEDiff. (a) In Stage 1, we pretrain a pruned VAE decoder that shares the latent space with SD and OSEDiff. (b) In Stage 2, we distill the knowledge from OSEDiff (\textcolor{blue}{ADC-teacher}) into AdcSR (\textcolor{blue}{ADC-student}) by aligning features in the pretrained decoder. An adversarial loss encourages the student to generate features that can fool a LoRA-finetuned SD UNet (\textcolor{blue}{ADC-discriminator}), utilizing the corresponding real features of GT images. Since all supervisions perform in the feature space, there is no need to decode images as in previous approaches \cite{wu2024one,zhang2024degradation}. (c) During inference, the LR image is directly fed into our trained compressed UNet and VAE decoder to obtain the super-resolution result.} \label{fig:method}
elements.sent-2411-065: OSEDiff \cite{wu2024one is a typical state-of-the-art one-step diffusion-based Real-ISR method that employs a LoRA-finetuned SD VAE encoder $\Enc_\text{OSEDiff}$, a LoRA-finetuned SD UNet $\Ep_\text{OSEDiff}$, a pretrained SD VAE decoder $\Dec_\text{SD}$, and a pretrained prompt extractor $\C$ \cite{wu2024seesr to perform super-resolution through the following process: \begin{equation}\z_LR = \Enc_OSEDiff(\x_LR), \quad \cbf = \C(\x_LR), \label{eq:osediff_encode_extract} \zhat_HR = \left[\z_LR - \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{T}} \Ep_OSEDiff(\z_LR;T,\cbf)\right]/{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{T}}}, \label{eq:osediff_denoising} \xhat_HR = \Dec_SD(\zhat_HR). \label{eq:osediff_decode}\end{equation} In Eq.~(\ref{eq:osediff_encode_extract ), the LR image $\x_\text{LR}$ is encoded into the VAE latent space, and text prompts $\cbf$ are extracted from $\x_\text{LR}$ in parallel.}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: In Eq.~(\ref{eq:osediff_denoising ), one-step diffusion denoising is executed using the noise schedule $\{\bar{\alpha _t\}$ \cite{ho2020denoising at the $T$-th timestep.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Finally, in Eq.~(\ref{eq:osediff_decode ), the denoised latent code is decoded back into image space to obtain the super-resolution image $\xhat_\text{HR}$.}
elements.sent-2411-068: However, OSEDiff has a total parameter number of 1775M and an inference latency of 0.11s on an NVIDIA A100 GPU for a $512 \times 512$ target HR image, which can still be too expensive for real deployment environments where both computational and storage resources are limited.
elements.sent-2411-069: Similar challenges persist in other one-step diffusion-based methods utilizing large-scale pretrained SD models \cite{cui2024taming,zhang2024degradation,noroozi2024you,li2024distillation,xie2024addsr .}
elements.sent-2411-070: To improve the efficiency of SD-based Real-ISR methods, we propose an Adversarial Diffusion Compression (ADC) framework.
elements.sent-2411-071: Its key insight is that ISR differs from T2I tasks, which rely solely on text inputs for generation, while the LR image in Real-ISR provides rich information about the target HR image.
elements.sent-2411-072: Thus, unlike previous SD-based one-step approaches that employ complete SD model structures, we hypothesize that competitive Real-ISR performance does not require these full architectures, which have been validated to possess sufficient capacity for one-step T2I and Real-ISR (see Sec.~\ref{sec:related_work ).}
elements.sent-2411-073: Taking OSEDiff \cite{wu2024one as example in this work, we propose that the modules used in Eqs.~(\ref{eq:osediff_encode_extract )-(\ref{eq:osediff_decode ) contain redundancy and can be removed or pruned for efficiency.}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: To be specific, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:teaser , we categorize the modules into two types: (1) Removable (VAE encoder, prompt extractor, text encoder, CA layers, and time embeddings) and (2) Prunable (denoising UNet and VAE decoder).}
elements.sent-2411-075: Based on this categorization, in ADC, we design a structural compression strategy that includes two modifications for SD-based one-step methods: (1) Removal of unnecessary modules, and (2) Pruning of remaining compressible modules.
elements.sent-2411-076: In the following, we detail and justify these modifications.
elements.sent-2411-078: In previous SD-based one-step Real-ISR approaches, the VAE encoder maps $\mathbf{x _\text{LR}$ to a latent code $\z_\text{LR}$, as shown in Eq.~(\ref{eq:osediff_encode_extract ).}}
elements.sent-2411-079: This process involves multiple downsampling operations, which can lead to the loss of information important for Real-ISR.
elements.sent-2411-080: To preserve the complete information of the LR input without loss, we eliminate the VAE encoder entirely.
elements.sent-2411-081: Instead, we apply a PixelUnshuffle \cite{shi2016real operation to $\mathbf{x _\text{LR}$, rearranging its spatial pixels into channel dimension while maintaining the same spatial size as $\z_\text{LR}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Correspondingly, the first convolution of UNet is adjusted to match the increased channel number, and the output of PixelUnshuffle is then directly input into the UNet.

elements.sent-2411-084: In models like OSEDiff, a prompt extractor generates textual prompts from $\mathbf{x _\text{LR}$, which are then used in text encoder and CA layers within the denoising UNet, as shown in Eqs.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Additionally, time embeddings are included to condition the UNet on different timesteps.
elements.sent-2411-086: While the text prompts are generally important for guiding T2I synthesis, in the specific context of Real-ISR, we have empirically observed that they contribute less significantly to enhance quality, compared to the other remaining modules.
elements.sent-2411-087: Furthermore, since OSEDiff performs only one-step diffusion sampling, time embeddings are unnecessary, as there is no need to differentiate between timesteps.
elements.sent-2411-088: Therefore, we remove the prompt extractor, text encoder, CA layers, and time embeddings from the UNet, retaining only its SA, linear, and convolutional layers.
elements.sent-2411-090: Before decoding the output image, traditional SD-based methods like OSEDiff map the high-capacity feature (often hundreds of channels) in UNet to a 4-channel latent code $\zhat_\text{HR}$.
elements.sent-2411-091: This dimensionality reduction can potentially result in a loss of feature information and constrain the model's representation ability.
elements.sent-2411-092: To mitigate this and fully leverage the rich feature representations learned by the UNet, we enhance the information flow between UNet and VAE decoder.
elements.sent-2411-093: Specifically, we remove the output layer of UNet and the input layer of VAE decoder, which reduce and then increase the feature channels.
elements.sent-2411-094: Instead, we introduce a convolution layer that directly connects the high-dimensional feature in UNet to the first blocks of the VAE decoder, improving the model's recovery quality while reducing its overall inference latency.. Pruning Feature Channels.
elements.sent-2411-095: We hypothesize that the current one-step model, compressed by the above three operations, still contains redundancy and has sufficient capacity to learn an effective Real-ISR mapping with even fewer parameters.
elements.sent-2411-096: Although previous works \cite{song2024sdxs,kim2023bk,castells2024edgefusion,zhang2024laptop,ma2024deepcache compress SD-based models by removing network blocks or layers, we find that this can noticeably degrade the performance of one-step diffusion networks, where the depth of UNet and VAE decoder is already relatively shallow.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Further decreasing the depth may impair the ability of model to extract hierarchical features and learn complex transformations for high-quality Real-ISR.
elements.sent-2411-098: To avoid this issue and strike a balance between recovery quality and efficiency, we opt for channel pruning.
elements.sent-2411-099: Concretely, we retain 75\% of the feature channels in the UNet and 50\% channels in the VAE decoder.
elements.sent-2411-100: This reduces the model's complexity while alleviating performance loss by preserving network depth.
elements.sent-2411-101: The resulting structurally compressed model, which we name AdcSR, incorporates the proposed two modifications in ADC and consists of three modules: (1) a PixelUnshuffle layer that prepares the LR input image $\x_\text{LR}$ for processing by rearranging its pixels without information loss; (2) a channel-pruned SD UNet without text encoder, CA layers, and time embeddings, processing the rearranged LR image while keeping the original depth; and (3) a channel-pruned VAE decoder which receives the high-dimensional features from UNet and generates the super-resolution image $\xhat_\text{HR}$.

elements.sent-2411-102: Direct removal and pruning can degrade the model's generative capabilities due to reduced capacity and altered network structure.
elements.sent-2411-103: To mitigate this, as Fig.~\ref{fig:method shows, our ADC uses a two-stage training scheme: (1) pretraining VAE decoder, and (2) adversarial distillation to compensate for potential performance loss and ensure high-quality Real-ISR.}
elements.sent-2411-105: In the first stage, we pretrain a pruned VAE decoder \cite{van2017neural,esser2021taming to restore its ability to decode images.}
elements.sent-2411-106: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:method (a), we freeze the parameters of the pretrained SD VAE encoder and train only the VAE decoder from scratch.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Given an input image $\x$, the encoder produces latent codes, which are then decoded back into an image $\xhat$ by the decoder.
elements.sent-2411-108: To train the decoder, following \cite{rombach2022high , we adopt a reconstruction loss consisting of a pixel-level $L_1$ loss $\lVert \xhat - \x \rVert_{1}$, an LPIPS loss \cite{zhang2018unreasonable , and a patch-based adversarial loss \cite{isola2017image,dosovitskiy2016generating,esser2021taming to encourage the reconstructed $\xhat$ to be visually similar to $\x$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-109: Stage 2: Knowledge Distillation with Adversarial Loss.
elements.sent-2411-110: In the second stage, we distill the knowledge from the pretrained OSEDiff (teacher) into our compressed AdcSR (student).
elements.sent-2411-111: Specifically, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:method (b), we connect the pruned UNet and all the first blocks of pruned decoder at the level with the smallest spatial size, and jointly finetune them.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The student is initialized using the pretrained SD and VAE decoder from Stage 1.
elements.sent-2411-113: Distillation is performed in the feature space by aligning the student's features $\f_{\text{student }$ with the teacher's corresponding features $\f_{\text{teacher }$ using an $L_1$ loss: \begin{equation}\Loss_{\text{distill}} = \lVert \mathbf{f}_{\text{student}} - \mathbf{f}_{\text{teacher}} \rVert_{1}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Here, $\f_{\text{teacher }$ is obtained by passing the LR image through the teacher's VAE encoder, prompt extractor, UNet, and all first blocks of the pretrained decoder, while $\f_{\text{student }$ is produced from the student's pruned UNet and all first blocks of the pruned decoder.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: This distillation in feature space is both effective and efficient without the need to decode images.
elements.sent-2411-116: To further enhance the visual quality of super-resolution outputs, we introduce an adversarial loss on $\f_{\text{student }$, encouraging it to follow the same distribution as the corresponding features of GT images.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Specifically, we obtain the real features $\f_{\text{GT }$ by encoding $\x_{\text{HR }$ using SD encoder and processing them with the first blocks of pruned decoder at the smallest spatial size.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: We reuse a pretrained SD UNet as the discriminator, where the first convolution layer is adjusted to match the channel number of $\f_{\text{student }$ and $\f_{\text{GT }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: In addition, we integrate LoRA modules, ensuring that only the LoRA and the first convolution layer remain trainable, while all other parameters are frozen to efficiently finetune the pretrained SD UNet.
elements.sent-2411-120: The discriminator is conditioned on the text prompts $\cbf$ extracted by the teacher, with timestep fixed at $T$.
elements.sent-2411-121: Following \cite{yin2024improved , we employ the non-saturating adversarial loss: \begin{equation}\Loss_\text{adv} = \text{Softplus} \left( -\text{Discriminator}(\f_\text{student}) \right),\end{equation} which provides fine-grained feedback as the discriminator's output shares the same spatial dimension as input features.}
elements.sent-2411-122: The total training loss is defined as $\Loss =\Loss_\text{distill + \lambda_\text{adv}\Loss_\text{adv}$.}
elements.sent-2411-123: Following \cite{wu2024seesr,wang2024sinsr,wu2024one,zhang2024degradation,sun2023improving,wang2024exploiting,yue2024resshift,lin2023diffbir,yang2023pixel,cui2024taming,li2024distillation,xie2024addsr , we conduct experiments on the Real-ISR task with scaling factor 4.}
elements.sent-2411-124: The sizes of LR and HR images are set to $128\times 128$ and $512\times 512$ by default.
elements.sent-2411-125: We initialize our pruned SD UNet using the pretrained weights of SD2.1-base \cite{sd21base , reusing only the parameters corresponding to the first 75\% of intermediate feature channels while removing the rest.}
elements.sent-2411-126: To match the $64\times 64$ spatial size of latent codes in SD, we set the scaling factor of PixelUnshuffle layer to 2.
elements.sent-2411-127: The convolutional kernels and biases in the first and last UNet layers are repeated in the channel dimension to align with the rearranged LR image and the intermediate features of the first blocks in our pruned SD VAE decoder.
elements.sent-2411-128: In Stage 1, we employ the code of latent diffusion models \cite{rombach2022high,ldm to pretrain a 50\% channel-pruned SD VAE decoder from scratch on OpenImage \cite{openimage for 250K steps, followed by 250K steps on LAION-Face \cite{laionface and LAION-Aesthetic \cite{laionaesthetics .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: The weighting factors of $L_1$ loss and LPIPS loss are both set to 1, while the weighting factor of the patch-based adversarial loss is set to 0 for the first 50K steps and 1 for the remaining steps.
elements.sent-2411-130: The learning rate is fixed at 1.3e-6.
elements.sent-2411-131: In Stage 2, we jointly finetune the 25\% channel-pruned UNet and all first blocks at the smallest spatial size of the pretrained VAE decoder from Stage 1 on LSDIR \cite{li2023lsdir with $\lambda_\text{adv =1$ for 200K steps.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: The learning rate is initialized at 1e-4 and halved for every 100K steps.
elements.sent-2411-133: The learning rate and LoRA rank for the discriminator are set to 1e-6 and 4, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-134: The high-order degradation pipeline of Real-ESRGAN \cite{wang2021real is used to synthesize LR-HR pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-135: In both two stages, we employ the Adam \cite{kingma2014adam optimizer and a batch size of 96 for training on 8 NVIDIA A100 (80GB) GPUs.. Test Datasets.}

elements.sent-2411-138: We adopt both full- and no-reference metrics for performance evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-139: For reference-based fidelity, we use PSNR and SSIM \cite{wang2004image , calculated on the Y channel in the YCrCb space.}
elements.sent-2411-140: For reference-based perceptual quality, we apply LPIPS \cite{zhang2018unreasonable and DISTS \cite{ding2020image .}}
elements.sent-2411-141: FID \cite{heusel2017gans is also employed to measure the distance between the distributions of GT and super-resolution images.}
elements.sent-2411-142: In addition, we utilize no-reference metrics including NIQE \cite{zhang2015feature , MUSIQ \cite{ke2021musiq , MANIQA \cite{yang2022maniqa , and CLIPIQA \cite{wang2023exploring .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: The first 8 columns of Tab.~\ref{tab:comp_diff_quantitative manifest that our AdcSR achieves promising results across multiple metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Firstly, it ranks in top 3 for full-reference quality metrics SSIM, LPIPS, and DISTS, surpassing most other approaches.
elements.sent-2411-145: Secondly, it attains competitive results in PSNR and no-reference metrics NIQE, MUSIQ, and CLIPIQA, performing on par with many state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-146: Thirdly, compared to the previous one-step diffusion-based models SinSR and particularly its teacher OSEDiff, AdcSR yields superiority in most of the perceptual quality metrics, and remains competitive with S3Diff across various cases.
elements.sent-2411-147: Figs.~\ref{fig:comp_one_step (top) and \ref{fig:comp_diff_qualitative exhibit the competitive performance of AdcSR in recovering sharp and photo-realistic images.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: We observe that StableSR, DiffBIR, SeeSR, and PASD can bring unnatural artifacts and blurriness at the intersection of the rocky landscape and the water, along with noise and distortions in the regions of leaves.
elements.sent-2411-149: ResShift and SinSR suffer from noticeable blurry artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-150: OSEDiff and S3Diff could generate fewer details on the surfaces of rocks and water, introducing an additional slight highlight effect on the cluster of leaves.
elements.sent-2411-151: In comparison, AdcSR effectively reconstructs vivid details and natural textures in the regions of parrot's feathers, building, rocky landscape, still water, and leaves.

elements.sent-2411-153: By distilling the SD-based one-step teacher \cite{wu2024one into a structurally compressed diffusion GAN, AdcSR offers substantial speedups: 383.3$\times$, 90.7$\times$, 143.3$\times$, 93.3$\times$, and 23.7$\times$ over previous multi-step approaches StableSR, DiffBIR, SeeSR, PASD, and ResShift, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Compared to the one-step model SinSR, it achieves a 4.3$\times$ acceleration.
elements.sent-2411-155: Compared to its teacher, the previously fastest method OSEDiff, it achieves a 3.7$\times$ acceleration, a 78\% reduction in computation, and a 74\% decrease in total parameters.
elements.sent-2411-156: This allows for a real-time speed of 34.79 frames per second (FPS) in diffusion-based Real-ISR.
elements.sent-2411-157: Notably, it attains a significant 9.3$\times$ speedup over S3Diff, which suffers from slower inferences due to its use of complete SD models and degradation-guided LoRA module.
elements.sent-2411-158: Fig.~\ref{fig:comp_complexity further visualizes this efficiency comparison using a bubble plot, exhibiting the effective compression and substantial efficiency gains of AdcSR while maintaining recovery quality.}

elements.sent-2411-189: In this paper, we proposed a novel method, AdcSR, based on our Adversarial Diffusion Compression (ADC) framework, for real-world image super-resolution (Real-ISR).
elements.sent-2411-190: To be specific, we structurally compressed a typical state-of-the-art SD-based one-step diffusion network, OSEDiff, into a smaller diffusion GAN.
elements.sent-2411-191: We identified and removed unnecessary modules (VAE encoder, prompt extractor, \etc) from OSEDiff, and pruned its remaining compressible modules (denoising UNet and VAE decoder).
elements.sent-2411-192: Since direct removal and pruning can degrade the model's generative capability, we developed a two-stage training scheme that first pretrains a pruned SD VAE decoder and then performs adversarial distillation to compensate for performance loss.
elements.sent-2411-193: Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrated that our AdcSR model delivered competitive image quality and superior computational efficiency compared to existing diffusion-based Real-ISR approaches.
elements.sent-2411-194: While ADC and AdcSR have demonstrated effectiveness in compressing SD-based one-step Real-ISR network and achieving real-time inference, they face challenges in accurately recovering fine textures and heavily degraded details, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:abla_effect_of_enc_and_opt .}
elements.sent-2411-195: Moreover, although this work focuses on streamlining the state-of-the-art Real-ISR model OSEDiff, our ADC framework could be extended to other SD-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-196: We plan to explore such extensions and integrate additional generative priors for Real-ISR in future work.. \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}
elements.sent-2411-197: Code and models are available at \url{https://github.com/Guaishou74851/AdcSR .}

elements.sent-2411-199: In addition to GANs, diffusion \cite{kim2024pagoda,song2020score,dhariwal2021diffusion,chen2024binarized -based methods such as SR3 \cite{saharia2022image , StableSR \cite{wang2024exploiting , and SeeSR \cite{wu2024seesr have enhanced the quality of super-resolved images by training powerful diffusion networks \cite{li2022srdiff,yue2024resshift,yue2024efficient,luo2023image,delbracio2023inversion,tang2024seeclear and leveraging pretrained text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models \cite{wang2024exploiting,yang2023pixel,lin2023diffbir,sun2023improving,yu2024scaling,qu2024xpsr,fanadadiffsr such as Stable Diffusion (SD) \cite{rombach2022high,stabilityai,podell2023sdxl,sd21base .}}}}}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Training and Inference of ADC} \label{alg} ~ \KwIn{Pretrained one-step teacher; Pretrained SD models: VAE encoder $\Enc_\text{SD}$, VAE decoder $\Dec_\text{SD}$, UNet $\Ep_{\text{SD}}$; Weighting factor $\lambda_\text{adv}$.} \BlankLine Stage 1: Pretraining Pruned VAE Decoder Prune the SD VAE decoder $\mathcal{D}_{\text{SD}}$ to obtain $\Dec_\text{pruned}$\; Initialize $\Dec_\text{pruned}$ and a discriminator as in \cite{rombach2022high,ldm}\; \For{number of training iterations}{ Sample a batch of images $\x$\; Obtain latent codes $\z = \Enc_\text{SD}(\x)$\; Reconstruct images $\xhat = \Dec_{\text{pruned}}(\z)$\; Compute reconstruction loss \cite{rombach2022high} of $\x$ and $\xhat$\; Update $\Dec_\text{pruned}$ using Adam optimizer\; Compute discriminator loss \cite{rombach2022high} of $\x$ and $\xhat$\; Update discriminator using Adam optimizer\; } \BlankLine ~ Stage 2: Adversarial Distillation Prune the SD UNet $\Ep_\text{SD}$ to obtain $\Ep_\text{pruned}$\; Initialize the student model using $\Ep_\text{pruned}$ and $\Dec_\text{pruned}$\; Initialize a feature-space discriminator using $\Ep_\text{SD}$\; \For{number of training iterations}{ Sample a batch of LR-HR pairs $(\x_{\text{LR}}, \x_\text{HR})$\; Compute features $\f_\text{student}$ from student model\; Compute features $\f_\text{teacher}$ from teacher model\; Compute distillation loss: \begin{equation}\Loss_distill = \lVert \f_student - \f_teacher \rVert_1\end{equation} Compute adversarial loss: \begin{equation}\Loss_adv = Softplus \left( -Discriminator(\f_student) \right)\end{equation} Compute total loss: $\Loss = \Loss_\text{distill} + \lambda_\text{adv} \Loss_\text{adv}$\; Update student model using Adam optimizer\; Compute features $\f_\text{GT}$ using $\x_\text{HR}$\; Compute discriminator loss: \begin{equation}\Loss_disc =~ Softplus \left(Discriminator(\f_student) \right) +~ Softplus \left( -Discriminator(\f_GT) \right)\end{equation} Update discriminator using Adam optimizer\; } \BlankLine ~ Inference\; Given LR image input $\x_\text{LR}$\; \Return Super-resolved image $\xhat_\text{HR} = \text{Student}(\x_\text{LR})$\;
elements.sent-2411-222: In Tab.~\ref{tab:comp_quantitative , we compare the proposed AdcSR model against twelve state-of-the-arts, including four representative GAN-based approaches: BSRGAN \cite{zhang2021designing , Real-ESRGAN \cite{wang2021real , LDL \cite{liang2022details , and FeMASR \cite{chen2022real , as well as eight diffusion-based methods \cite{wang2024exploiting,lin2023diffbir,wu2024seesr,yang2023pixel,yue2024resshift,wang2024sinsr,wu2024one,zhang2024degradation across three synthetic and real-world test datasets, evaluated using nine metrics \cite{wang2004image,zhang2018unreasonable,ding2020image,heusel2017gans,zhang2015feature,ke2021musiq,yang2022maniqa,wang2023exploring .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: We observe that, firstly, the traditional GAN-based approaches generally perform well on reference-based metrics, particularly the fidelity measures PSNR and SSIM.
elements.sent-2411-224: Secondly, diffusion-based methods outperform these GANs in most perceptual quality metrics, showing their ability to better generate natural textures.
elements.sent-2411-225: Thirdly, AdcSR achieves competitive results, surpassing its teacher OSEDiff in most cases, which validates the effectiveness of ADC's compression and adversarial distillation.
elements.sent-2411-226: Figs.~\ref{fig:comp_qualitative_DIV2K_Val , \ref{fig:comp_qualitative_DRealSR , and \ref{fig:comp_qualitative_RealSR present visual comparisons across super-resolution images produced by these approaches.}}}
elements.sent-2411-227: We observe that, firstly, GAN-based approaches generally show weaker generative capabilities than diffusion-based methods, recovering fewer details overall.
elements.sent-2411-228: Secondly, traditional multi-step SD-based methods generate rich details but may introduce artifacts, such as those observed on the spiky texture of the inflated pufferfish by StableSR, DiffBIR, SeeSR, and PASD.
elements.sent-2411-229: Thirdly, ResShift and SinSR tend to produce oversmoothed results in areas of the leaves and red flower petals, where the vein structures and textures are less distinct.
elements.sent-2411-230: This may be due to their lack of exploiting the powerful SD priors.
elements.sent-2411-231: Fourthly, AdcSR demonstrates competitive performance, generating natural and balanced details in the pufferfish and leaves, comparable to OSEDiff and S3Diff, which can subtly introduce an additional slight highlight effect on the cluster of leaves.
elements.sent-2411-232: These results comprehensively confirm the effectiveness of our approach in compressing SD-based models for Real-ISR while maintaining quality.

elements.figure-2411-025: \setlength{\tabcolsep}{2pt} \caption{Efficiency comparison on a flagship mobile device, \textbf{Qualcomm SM8750 (Snapdragon 8 Gen 4)}, for super-resolving an LR input image of size $128\times 128$ with a scaling factor of 4.} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|ccc} \shline Method Latency (ms)â†“ Memory (MB)â†“ Storage (MB)â†“ OSEDiff 1647 1777 1693 AdcSR (Ours) \best{65} \best{510} \best{435} Reduction Rate (\%) 97 71 74 \shline \end{tabular}} \label{tab:efficiency_comp_mobile}
elements.sent-2411-239: We conduct an efficiency comparison of the proposed AdcSR method against its teacher model, OSEDiff, on a flagship mobile platform, Qualcomm SM8750 (Snapdragon 8 Gen 4) \cite{qsp , utilizing the Hexagon Digital Signal Processor (DSP).}
elements.sent-2411-240: All models are evaluated using the Qualcomm AI Engine Direct Software Development Kit (SDK) \cite{sdk with 8-bit weights and 16-bit activations (W8A16) quantization for fair comparison.}
elements.sent-2411-241: The results reported in Tab.~\ref{tab:efficiency_comp_mobile demonstrate that AdcSR significantly outperforms OSEDiff in both speed and resource efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-242: Specifically, AdcSR achieves a 25$\times$ acceleration in inference latency, reduces memory footprint by 71\% (from 1.7GB to 0.5GB), and decreases storage requirements by 74\% (from 1.7GB to 0.4GB).
elements.sent-2411-243: These savings are substantial for practical deployment on mobile devices, where resources are typically constrained.
elements.sent-2411-244: To summarize, AdcSR advances beyond previous SD-based one-step Real-ISR models, providing a more efficient, cost-effective solution for real mobile applications.. \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}
